note_data.title: Chemical energetics
keyPoints: Endothermic reaction means heat or energy ENTER (absorbed/gained) to break bond
Exothermic means heat, energy EXIT (give out/lose) to form bonds
Melting (Solid → Liquid) - Particles gain energy, vibrate more, occurs at melting point, endothermic reaction because particles gain energy to break bonds
Freezing (Liquid → Solid) - Occurs at freezing point (also the m.p. of pure substance), exothermic reaction because particles give out energy to form bonds
For both Freezing & Melting, temp. remains constant during the process
Crystallization - Formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/saturated solution
Distillation - Obtaining pure liquid from solution, constant temperature of process = Boiling Point of pure liquid
Filtration possible because liquid particles small enough to go through filter paper pores, solid particles too large
Pure substances have fixed composition, fixed Mp/Bp, produces only 1 spot on chromatogram, all molecules same (equal)
Mixtures have variable composition, variable Bp/Mp, 2 or more spots on chromatogram, 2 or more different molecules

definitions.definition: heat or energy ENTER (absorbed/gained) to break bond
heat, energy EXIT (give out/lose) to form bonds
no more solid can dissolve in it
Liquid obtained and collected in conical flask during distillation

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the observation and energy change that occurs

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the combustion of methane is an exothermic reaction in terms of bond breaking and bond forming.
modelAnswer.answer: Bond breaking is endothermic and requires energy. Bond forming is exothermic and releases energy. In combustion of methane, the energy released by forming new bonds is greater than the energy required to break existing bonds.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Bond breaking requires energy/is endothermic
Mark 2: Bond forming releases energy/is exothermic
Mark 3: Energy released > energy required/net energy released

commonMistakes: Confusing endothermic and exothermic - remember EN means energy ENTERs, EX means energy EXITs
Thinking temperature changes during melting and freezing processes when it remains constant
Not recognizing that freezing point is also the melting point of pure substance
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystallization'
Listen to instrumental musics for performance improvement - ideal musics such as 'Free as a bird' and 'A day with you' by Omar, using earpiece NOT speakers
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Energy profile diagram showing reactants at higher level than products (exothermic) with activation energy peak, or products higher than reactants (endothermic) with activation energy barrier clearly marked
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate enthalpy change using bond energies and explain why experimental values differ from calculated values
N/D 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) tested drawing and interpreting energy level diagrams for exothermic and endothermic reactions with activation energy labeled
M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) was a multiple choice question requiring students to identify which reaction had the most negative enthalpy change from given data
N/D 2023 P2 Q5 (7 marks) examined calorimetry calculations where students had to use q=mcΔT and calculate enthalpy change per mole of reactant

workedExample.question: Use the bond energies below to calculate the enthalpy change for the reaction: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Bond energies (kJ/mol): C-H = 413, O=O = 498, C=O = 805, H-O = 464. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify bonds broken in reactants - CH₄ has 4 C-H bonds, 2O₂ has 2 O=O bonds
Step 2: Calculate energy required to break bonds = (4 × 413) + (2 × 498) = 1652 + 996 = 2648 kJ/mol
Step 3: Identify bonds formed in products - CO₂ has 2 C=O bonds, 2H₂O has 4 H-O bonds
Step 4: Calculate energy released forming bonds = (2 × 805) + (4 × 464) = 1610 + 1856 = 3466 kJ/mol
Step 5: Calculate enthalpy change = Energy required - Energy released = 2648 - 3466 = -818 kJ/mol
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct bonds broken calculation, 1 mark for correct bonds formed calculation, 1 mark for correct subtraction method, 1 mark for final answer with correct sign

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the signs in enthalpy calculations' — always remember that energy released gives negative ΔH values for exothermic reactions
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to include units in calorimetry calculations' — always include kJ/mol for enthalpy changes and show all unit conversions clearly
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates struggled with activation energy concepts' — remember that catalysts lower activation energy but do not change the overall enthalpy change of the reaction
note_data.keyDiagram: Energy level diagram showing reactants and products on y-axis (energy) vs reaction progress on x-axis. Must include: horizontal lines for reactant and product energy levels, curved arrow showing activation energy (Ea) from reactants to peak, overall enthalpy change (ΔH) as vertical arrow between reactant and product levels, labels for 'activated complex' at the peak. For exothermic reactions, products are lower than reactants (ΔH negative). Include second curve for catalyzed reaction with lower activation energy peak.
note_data.quickRecap: Energy changes occur during state changes - melting is endothermic (energy enters to break bonds) while freezing is exothermic (energy exits to form bonds). Temperature remains constant during both processes. Separation techniques depend on the properties of substances - filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solids from solutions, and distillation to obtain pure liquids.
